title: "Drawing" the Mona Lisa with GPT-5.6, Claude, Gemini, and Grok
excerpt: As I looked through the images I was unimpressed entirely, at first. But, then I started thinking, these look a little... "childish" to me.
content_html:
As I looked through the images I was unimpressed entirely, at first. But, then I started thinking, these look a little... "childish" to me.

Childish as in... A newish artist who is drawing a concept rather than light / forms (Which is something artists typically do as they understand drawing more and more).

The rose in the vase specifically - some models understood that there was supposed to be shading, reflections, the concept of refraction - others just drew "blue = glass" and "green = stem" and "red = rose".

Really odd to look at, considering if I saw any of these drawings from a human kid, I would say "good job buddy" and put it on the fridge. I'm expecting these to get better as models improve, and perhaps the artistic progression will be there along with it...


I call this "symbol drawing". Beginning artists do this. They think "This is a head, a head is round. This is where eyes go, eyes are shaped like this", and the whole thing ends up being a collage of symbols vs a representation of the space and experience of viewing a face. When I used to be OK at drawing, it was because I forced myself to use touch instead of sight to compose images. So weird to explain, but I'd feel the 3d to get the lighting and such better.

A lot of art that someone smarter than me told me to appreciate seems to follow the pattern of hitting the space and/or experience while minimizing the use of symbols. Impressionistic paintings esp avoid symbols IMHO, while bizzaro picassos abuse symbols outright and still hit the experience they are going for.


Really odd to look at, considering if I saw any of these drawings from a human kid, I would say "good job buddy" and put it on the fridge.

The Grok ones in particular gave me that thought. Most of them really look like what a kid would do when given the same tools, while the other models' output have distinctly more "AI-ness" to them (for lack of a better term.)


I took the same prompts to Gemini and was stunned by the results. The are completely different from the images shown in the article (and genuinely good art pieces that were generated).


Were you using the Gemini image generator though? The article is about using LLMs to do drawing via tool calls.


What's interesting is that the way in which they're childish is actually extremely human. In fact, one of the ways that you're often taught to draw more realistically is to stop thinking of the concepts as icons you're drawing the outlines of, and instead sort of blur your eyes and see things as they are: hues and values. In other words, become a camera or a printer that has no idea what it's capturing or printing other than a grid of values. That is how you achieve realism.

The fact that it has clearly iconified these concepts in its mind and is tracing the outlines of the things it thinks/expects to go where is very human.


Yes - precisely what I was getting at with the childish comment.

It felt a bit surreal to see a machines rendition of something that very closely maps to a younger human as they explore + understand more about the world. Scary, even (to me). This was the first time I’ve seen LLM output and thought “wow, maybe it is learning.”


Parrots can also sound extremely human, but it’s only mimicry.

How do we know the difference?


Yes, but Parrots don't mimic the stages of learning speech development like children do. They just memorize a phrase.

These SOTA LLMs aren't trying to mimic existing children's drawings, but interestingly they're following somewhat similar progression that human children do as they develop.


They don’t develop. We should stop anthropomorphizing LLMs.

AI labs are improving the ML techniques used to build better models; it’s a big difference.


That's very clearly what they're referring to also, or at least I certainly doubt this would be lost on them, or most anyone here, at this point.


I imagine that it should be clear to everyone here now, until I read threads like this comparing child development to the advances in LLM building.

It’s like saying that a new M-Apple chip developed like a child. There is a clear tendency to give human like attributes to LLMs, probably caused by the terms used in ML: training, learning, etc.


I'm not sure about that.

For me, it was the same genre of comment as e.g. comments that call out how vision models tend to be structured surprisingly similarly to how the visual cortex is structured, even without specifically attempting to make that happen, and within the boundaries of our understanding of either things of course.

So not so much anthropomorphization, and more a recognition of the unintentional biomimicry.

Though to be honest, I'll be damned if any of the pictures in the post look anything like any child drawing I've ever seen, Grok's or any of the other models.


LLMs come complete they don't train on the fly based on interaction with the world. LLMs are wikipedia if they stopped allowing edits and all of the previous edits are training.


Both statements are true without contradiction.

A specific model develops as it passes through training.

AI labs change the architecture between models to allow them to surpass the previous models' best scores.

It's also going to keep being a hard sell to say "stop anthropomorphizing LLMs" when the models anthropomorphise themselves.

But besides that: Yeah, sure, they're not human, they're a cargo-cult mimicry of by and of minds that popped out of evolution doing gradient decent on intergenerational survivability. So what? Still interesting when the result of such cargo-culting incidentally echoes what our natural-selection-not-engineered moist electrochemistry happens to do.


If you want to know if something is a human or a parrot, you could give it a paint brush and ask it to paint the Mona Lisa.


you can also use your eyes, hands or other senses :').

reminds me of those jokes?

what is difference between a parot and a human? silence rly? u cant tell the difference between a parot and a human? :')


My parrot doesn't have a take, but it sure can repeat one it heard on Reddit.


So, you're saying that it was somebody on Reddit who disproved the conjecture, and not a "stochastic parrot?"


You asked for my parrot's take, not how to disprove a conjecture.

Edit: And not a great example to use to prove human-ness considering humans didn't disprove the conjecture?


GPT 5.6 Sol had the best two drawings (rose and starry nights) but even more impressive was how efficient it was RE cost/time/tokens vs Fable (3.4M vs 14.6M / $7.74 vs $161!). OpenAI has quietly innovated around inference - this is will be a growing differentiator even against open models.


I do think they are going to stretch their lead in value if Anthropic doesn't wake up and stop YOLOing tokens. Kimi is an amazing achievement, but it has the same (or worse) kitchen sink approach as Fable.

At work, even if Fable is technically better I much prefer Sol because it is so much faster and concise.


For me personally, there is no point in using more reasoning than medium or sometimes high. It just gets so much slower and expensive, meaning that i have to wait longer to evaluate the results and provide feedback or continue to the next task.


Grok! LOL!

Seriously, what's going on there ? Why is it so different from others? Is it just behind technologically/training wise or it's using something fundamentally different?


Grok 4.5 is...something else.

It performs much better than composer2.5 (while being as fast). It's not Opus, but I think it's not that far off. Definitely better than sonnet for what I've been doing.

On the other hand, I think they probably heavily adapted the training data so that it really is extremely focused on code. I just recently ran my personal "poetry benchmark" on it (where I give it ~850 poems I've written over my life and ask it to comment the corpus as a whole), and it's whack. It tries to write in portuguese (most of the poems are portuguese) and code-switches constantly and mixes up words to the point of making what it writes almost unreadable (e.g. it writes stuff like "You can't QoS that that look for beast poems", in portuguese, all messed up). The quality of the analysis is also quite bad (I'd say it's definitely behind Sonnet).

So I really think they either threw away data that wasn't tied to coding so that they could fine-tune it to that, or somehow they've got such an unbalanced dataset that coding ends up dominating either way. To me, its disastrous performance in this drawing "competition" fits this narrative.


Some of my poetry has clear IT jargon, but it's a very small portion of it (<1%). Some of my teenage poetry revolved a lot around the idea of wanting to become science, knowledge, and machine, and be rid of feeling altogether (to become an idea that has no body, or to be come the mathematical equations that define the world), with some very amateur odes written glorifying science and machine (a clear pastiche of Álvaro de Campos with a modern twist). But, again, this is not the majority of the work, far from it.

For some reason, OpenAI models, Gemini (and apparently Grok too), love to latch onto this and obsess over this idea that it's "programming poetry" or "poetry for the IT crowd". Often OpenAI and Gemini try to write the "equations of my poetry" (granted, I do write about a cyclical relationship between thinking, feeling and writing a lot, and I do have ONE poem which ends with a Q.E.D.).

I'm giving this context to say that it is very bizarre. It's as if they latch onto it and act as if it's a core or highly distinguished part of the poetry, when it really isn't. Anthropic models, on the other hand, absolutely do not do this, and have never done it.

I really don't understand why this happens. Maybe it's because it has a lot of portuguese, I don't know. And even though the "QoS" is clearly the wrong token being generated, I have had situations where gemini spoke of some phase of my poetry as the "Q&A part" (really, no joke...)

In any case, it's why it's my personal benchmark after all :D


might also be U shaped memory issue did you try changing the order of your poems to see if it focusses on different ones? maybe it just happened to have those old ones in points it memory was focused at. (ofc its not good but would be an alternative idea to it focussing on IT things/code to produce such results)


Yep, I did. It didn’t have any effect. Randomizing the poems has stopped having a significant effect in the last year or so on the analysis as a whole.

It still somewhat affects the way the AI looks at the poems, especially if it has to make lists of the “best”, but it is not a very pronounced effect nowadays. Maybe it gives some preference for earlier and latter poems but not a lot.

Back when I started doing it, these holes in the context were super obvious, just like you described. It would mixup entire periods and also hallucinate or forget about specific sections. That’s precisely why I started randomizing them as part of my experiments.

Nowadays the frontier models are much better and don’t really mix anything up.


Did Elon tell some poor engineer to give grok a prompt injection for drawing,"make it look like one of my childhood drawings!" Just like the Tesla truck?


The razor-wire at the bottom for Starry Night was clever, and very Grok. Really shows its military spirit.

Edit: I just don't see the point of redacting the Mona Lisa


Is this not someone being near infinitely obtuse?

HN, the only environment in the world where people will scientifically falsify a joke.


ESL?

Allow me to explain the theory behind the humor...

Grok's art had heavy use of what strongly resembles a permanent marker with lateral-ish scribbles similar to what one might see when someone is trying to mark something as discarded. Documents, especially those procured by the US government when responding to FOIA requests, contain many 'strike-through' style black marks resembling bold permanent-marker lines, which obfuscate the words they wish the reader to remain ignorant of. In many cases, this is the entire document, especially where unredacted words might otherwise serve in any way to clarify the nature of the FOIA inquiry, i.e. answer the question.

Grok's art, resembled to me, and others potentially appreciating the joke, redactions more than art. In some forms of humor, the presenter of the joke feigns obtuseness, or a mildly self-deprecating, stupid perspective. But the stupidity of the perspective actually reveals a relevant concept, or idea.

Often, such jokes can be deciphered using common sense, or various razors, including both occam and hanlon, the former self-suggesting that the joker is probably not so stupid as to think Grok was really redacting anything, and the latter self-suggesting that there was probably no malice involved.


I read the balls as “houses”, though the phallic spire emerging from them dead-center is also very on-brand


I must admit, I have a natural tendency to overlook C&Bs, but solid catch -- it's there. Perhaps that explains the Mona Lisa redaction; Grok probably put more effort into that one.


The Grok ones are amusing, almost comically bad. However, whenever I've tried to pass an image creation request to any of the Opus models, it's been far worse, like first week of using Microsoft Paint bad (while ChatGPT would create social media quality images using the same prompts)


Opus doesn't have access to an image model, while ChatGPT does (it's not multi-modal, but it can prompt OpenAI's image model). So Opus by default is forced to write code to generate images, or generate SVG's as text. That's putting it in a worse situation than the linked article, where all the models were given rudimentary tools.

Give Opus access to an image model, and it will use that to create images just fine just like ChatGPT.


The Grok ones are so weird that they cross into uncanny and surreal. Truly bizarre and capable of eliciting feelings from me, if only bad feelings…


The most interesting result for me is that they apparently prompted the models to optimize for SSIM, but many of the models trend worse over time. I suppose because viewing the canvas always comes after drawing, and they didn't give "revert to previous" capability as part of the toolkit.

Which in turn kinda jives with my experience of using these models for code: to some extent they only seem to have a concept of "forward", which invariably leads to "write more code to fix previous problems created", rather than taking a step back and removing broken things entirely.


Stopping problem at work. I wonder if results would improve if we gave the agents an “undo” or “revert” tool.


GPT-5.6 Sol is the knock out here. Some of those results are really human/charming.

The rectangular smudge tool is a weird tool in the first place, but it's cute to see the models try to use it.


Its rose is also insane. Not just the shading of the flower but its clever use of the smudge tool to soften the background and then soften the vase so it looked more like glass.

And I like how its starry night decided to accentuate the church/steeple in the village backdrop.

Pretty cool how it did these with a scrappy little tool palette. What a fun idea.


I found it funny that Fable reinterpreted the Starry Night as "an oil-well in a desert", while Grok went for a "Stalin statue in a prison camp" angle.


A year or so ago I was trying to get llms to make pixel art by asking them to create a 2d array of color codes. I'm no prompt guru but they all failed at anything beyond a basic shape. I tried at many resolutions from 16x16, 32x32, 64x64, and maybe a little larger but basically only then quit small ones were even recognizeable.


This is picking the wrong tool for the job twice over. Half of it is seeing if text-to-image mechanics exist in general purpose LLMs, the other half is seeing if they can reason their way about generating the 2d array. Imagine asking this of yourself and doing it through just a train of thought. Yes, I could probably draw a basic smiley face in the color of your choice in a 16x16 without antialiasing in 15 minutes.


Perhaps there was something in the prompt or settings preventing this, but I'm surprised (and slightly disappointed) that none of the models approached this the way I would: download an image of the Mona Lisa, run all of the drawing functions many times to construct a forward model of the drawing implements, and attempt to solve some kind of explicit inverse problem through either ML or a classical algorithm to minimize some difference metric. Were they just restricted from running code or are the models unindustrious without a very specific prompt?


Weird choice of SSIM/RMSE. By feeding these back into the model, the agent is actively degrading its artistic output. This is only valid for the target reference I think?

Alternatively, I think much better results could be had by computing the cosine similarly using DINOv2 ONNX (@xenova/transformers).

My guess is that the results are greatly limited by relying on the current metrics.

Even better, attach toModelOutput directly to drawTool so it returns the rendered canvas image immediately upon drawing.


Where do these models even have this data to learn from. There must be massive computer use datasets? I have a hard time believing it’s emergent if it’s able to do something this good.


If it is, it's a horrible ad. My main takeaway is that all of them were god awful at image generation and Fable was 20x more expensive and still awful.


I'm surprised that the harness wasn't better described here. Is the draw tool really just asking LLMs to provide a set of coordinates/parameters for generating shapes? Honestly, if so, the results are shockingly good. Think of how well a typical human artist would do with such a crude method of applying brushstrokes.


I think a lot better

- the best image related stuff I've seen is where the harness is constantly cropping and looking closer at things (likely helps a lot for computer vision in general) - also it would be interesting because the harness could almost have its own "palette" as if it could play with blank squares and different strokes over lapping or blending before applying to the main canvas


Claude was clearly 'pushing back' on the coziness of the cabin. But I think it did best with the cat. Grok, I fear, is making a case for euthanasia. It's suffering and I think it would be cruel to let it continue. Someone pull the plug. .


Rather the difference of cost of Claude is pretty incredible. Then again, I guess they gotta make it rain while they can.


He mentions they used quite different methods to get to the end-result. I'd love to know how much changing the prompt impacts things: like telling claude to only draw and blend, similar to sol, etc.

There's likely not training data for this and Claude seemed to spend a lot of time blending the background.


I just tried with Deepseek and got this for the Mona Lisa

           .-""-.
          /      \
         |  _  _  |
         | (o)(o) |
         \   /\   /
          |  --  |
          |  \/  |
          |      |
         /   --   \
        /  /    \  \
       /  /      \  \
      (__/        \__)

It might not have been the most scientific test


Now this is a cool test. Sol's the best in each one, while Grok looks like the work of that person who "fixed" that Jesus painting..


Not useless. LLMs are the most general purpose computer algorithms ever created. They are getting smarter and cheaper at a geometric rate. What is a bad idea today could have useful applications tomorrow.


> cheaper at a geometric rate

Citation needed - my company is paying more than ever for code generation. I have no reason to believe (given anecdotes) that anyone finds themselves in the opposite situation.


You’re paying more in total because the overall cost has gone down. This is normal economics when something useful gets cheaper. If you were to use original GPT-4 to do as much, you’d be paying an order of magnitude more.


It keeps getting cheaper and better -- for me.

I get a lot more use (read: cheaper per interaction) and much better quality results from the $20 that I spend on this stuff every month than I did several years ago.

(And several years before that, it was all essentially unobtanium.)


I'm missing some context here.

First of all, this is a marketing piece, made for engagement.

Second, in production, you would adjust your approach to the model's strengths and weaknesses. For example, if you found that a model does not respond to the tools you are offering, then you would find a way to make it more responsive. Also, we know that different models handle prompts and system prompts differently. So comparing several models from different providers against exactly the same prompt is quite a naive way of exploring the capabilities of those models or comparing them to each other. You may have just happened to “speak the language“ of one model better than that of another. Why would that be the model’s fault and not yours?

Third, none of these results mean anything if you do not run each process on each model several times to check for consistency. Your model might just have gotten lucky. This point is even more true when you're talking about a progressive task where one step builds on the previous one. The model might just have painted itself into a corner.

I read the balls as “houses”, though the phallic spire emerging from them dead-center is also very on-brand I must admit, I have a natural tendency to overlook C&Bs, but solid catch -- it's there. Perhaps that explains the Mona Lisa redaction; Grok probably put more effort into that one. The Grok ones are amusing, almost comically bad. However, whenever I've tried to pass an image creation request to any of the Opus models, it's been far worse, like first week of using Microsoft Paint bad (while ChatGPT would create social media quality images using the same prompts) Opus doesn't have access to an image model, while ChatGPT does (it's not multi-modal, but it can prompt OpenAI's image model). So Opus by default is forced to write code to generate images, or generate SVG's as text. That's putting it in a worse situation than the linked article, where all the models were given rudimentary tools.Give Opus access to an image model, and it will use that to create images just fine just like ChatGPT. The Grok ones are so weird that they cross into uncanny and surreal. Truly bizarre and capable of eliciting feelings from me, if only bad feelings… The most interesting result for me is that they apparently prompted the models to optimize for SSIM, but many of the models trend worse over time. I suppose because viewing the canvas always comes after drawing, and they didn't give "revert to previous" capability as part of the toolkit.Which in turn kinda jives with my experience of using these models for code: to some extent they only seem to have a concept of "forward", which invariably leads to "write more code to fix previous problems created", rather than taking a step back and removing broken things entirely. Stopping problem at work. I wonder if results would improve if we gave the agents an “undo” or “revert” tool. GPT-5.6 Sol is the knock out here. Some of those results are really human/charming.The rectangular smudge tool is a weird tool in the first place, but it's cute to see the models try to use it. Its rose is also insane. Not just the shading of the flower but its clever use of the smudge tool to soften the background and then soften the vase so it looked more like glass.And I like how its starry night decided to accentuate the church/steeple in the village backdrop.Pretty cool how it did these with a scrappy little tool palette. What a fun idea. I found it funny that Fable reinterpreted the Starry Night as "an oil-well in a desert", while Grok went for a "Stalin statue in a prison camp" angle. A year or so ago I was trying to get llms to make pixel art by asking them to create a 2d array of color codes. I'm no prompt guru but they all failed at anything beyond a basic shape. I tried at many resolutions from 16x16, 32x32, 64x64, and maybe a little larger but basically only then quit small ones were even recognizeable. This is picking the wrong tool for the job twice over. Half of it is seeing if text-to-image mechanics exist in general purpose LLMs, the other half is seeing if they can reason their way about generating the 2d array. Imagine asking this of yourself and doing it through just a train of thought. Yes, I could probably draw a basic smiley face in the color of your choice in a 16x16 without antialiasing in 15 minutes. Perhaps there was something in the prompt or settings preventing this, but I'm surprised (and slightly disappointed) that none of the models approached this the way I would: download an image of the Mona Lisa, run all of the drawing functions many times to construct a forward model of the drawing implements, and attempt to solve some kind of explicit inverse problem through either ML or a classical algorithm to minimize some difference metric. Were they just restricted from running code or are the models unindustrious without a very specific prompt? Weird choice of SSIM/RMSE. By feeding these back into the model, the agent is actively degrading its artistic output. This is only valid for the target reference I think?Alternatively, I think much better results could be had by computing the cosine similarly using DINOv2 ONNX (@xenova/transformers).My guess is that the results are greatly limited by relying on the current metrics.Even better, attach toModelOutput directly to drawTool so it returns the rendered canvas image immediately upon drawing. Where do these models even have this data to learn from. There must be massive computer use datasets? I have a hard time believing it’s emergent if it’s able to do something this good. If it is, it's a horrible ad. My main takeaway is that all of them were god awful at image generation and Fable was 20x more expensive and still awful. I'm surprised that the harness wasn't better described here. Is the draw tool really just asking LLMs to provide a set of coordinates/parameters for generating shapes? Honestly, if so, the results are shockingly good. Think of how well a typical human artist would do with such a crude method of applying brushstrokes. I think a lot better- the best image related stuff I've seen is where the harness is constantly cropping and looking closer at things (likely helps a lot for computer vision in general) - also it would be interesting because the harness could almost have its own "palette" as if it could play with blank squares and different strokes over lapping or blending before applying to the main canvas Claude was clearly 'pushing back' on the coziness of the cabin. But I think it did best with the cat. Grok, I fear, is making a case for euthanasia. It's suffering and I think it would be cruel to let it continue. Someone pull the plug. . Rather the difference of cost of Claude is pretty incredible. Then again, I guess they gotta make it rain while they can. He mentions they used quite different methods to get to the end-result. I'd love to know how much changing the prompt impacts things: like telling claude to only draw and blend, similar to sol, etc.There's likely not training data for this and Claude seemed to spend a lot of time blending the background. I just tried with Deepseek and got this for the Mona Lisa .-""-. / \ | _ _ | | (o)(o) | \ /\ / | -- | | \/ | | | / -- \ / / \ \ / / \ \ (__/ \__) It might not have been the most scientific test Now this is a cool test. Sol's the best in each one, while Grok looks like the work of that person who "fixed" that Jesus painting.. Not useless. LLMs are the most general purpose computer algorithms ever created. They are getting smarter and cheaper at a geometric rate. What is a bad idea today could have useful applications tomorrow. > cheaper at a geometric rateCitation needed - my company is paying more than ever for code generation. I have no reason to believe (given anecdotes) that anyone finds themselves in the opposite situation. You’re paying more in total because the overall cost has gone down. This is normal economics when something useful gets cheaper. If you were to use original GPT-4 to do as much, you’d be paying an order of magnitude more. It keeps getting cheaper and better -- for me.I get a lot more use (read: cheaper per interaction) and much better quality results from the $20 that I spend on this stuff every month than I did several years ago.(And several years before that, it was all essentially unobtanium.) I'm missing some context here.First of all, this is a marketing piece, made for engagement.Second, in production, you would adjust your approach to the model's strengths and weaknesses. For example, if you found that a model does not respond to the tools you are offering, then you would find a way to make it more responsive. Also, we know that different models handle prompts and system prompts differently. So comparing several models from different providers against exactly the same prompt is quite a naive way of exploring the capabilities of those models or comparing them to each other. You may have just happened to “speak the language“ of one model better than that of another. Why would that be the model’s fault and not yours?Third, none of these results mean anything if you do not run each process on each model several times to check for consistency. Your model might just have gotten lucky. This point is even more true when you're talking about a progressive task where one step builds on the previous one. The model might just have painted itself into a corner.

blocks.html_en:
As I looked through the images I was unimpressed entirely, at first. But, then I started thinking, these look a little... "childish" to me.

Childish as in... A newish artist who is drawing a concept rather than light / forms (Which is something artists typically do as they understand drawing more and more).

The rose in the vase specifically - some models understood that there was supposed to be shading, reflections, the concept of refraction - others just drew "blue = glass" and "green = stem" and "red = rose".

Really odd to look at, considering if I saw any of these drawings from a human kid, I would say "good job buddy" and put it on the fridge. I'm expecting these to get better as models improve, and perhaps the artistic progression will be there along with it...

blocks.html_en:
I call this "symbol drawing". Beginning artists do this. They think "This is a head, a head is round. This is where eyes go, eyes are shaped like this", and the whole thing ends up being a collage of symbols vs a representation of the space and experience of viewing a face. When I used to be OK at drawing, it was because I forced myself to use touch instead of sight to compose images. So weird to explain, but I'd feel the 3d to get the lighting and such better.

A lot of art that someone smarter than me told me to appreciate seems to follow the pattern of hitting the space and/or experience while minimizing the use of symbols. Impressionistic paintings esp avoid symbols IMHO, while bizzaro picassos abuse symbols outright and still hit the experience they are going for.

Really odd to look at, considering if I saw any of these drawings from a human kid, I would say "good job buddy" and put it on the fridge.

The Grok ones in particular gave me that thought. Most of them really look like what a kid would do when given the same tools, while the other models' output have distinctly more "AI-ness" to them (for lack of a better term.)

blocks.html_en:
What's interesting is that the way in which they're childish is actually extremely human. In fact, one of the ways that you're often taught to draw more realistically is to stop thinking of the concepts as icons you're drawing the outlines of, and instead sort of blur your eyes and see things as they are: hues and values. In other words, become a camera or a printer that has no idea what it's capturing or printing other than a grid of values. That is how you achieve realism.

The fact that it has clearly iconified these concepts in its mind and is tracing the outlines of the things it thinks/expects to go where is very human.

I imagine that it should be clear to everyone here now, until I read threads like this comparing child development to the advances in LLM building.

It’s like saying that a new M-Apple chip developed like a child. There is a clear tendency to give human like attributes to LLMs, probably caused by the terms used in ML: training, learning, etc.

blocks.html_en:
I'm not sure about that.

For me, it was the same genre of comment as e.g. comments that call out how vision models tend to be structured surprisingly similarly to how the visual cortex is structured, even without specifically attempting to make that happen, and within the boundaries of our understanding of either things of course.

So not so much anthropomorphization, and more a recognition of the unintentional biomimicry.

Though to be honest, I'll be damned if any of the pictures in the post look anything like any child drawing I've ever seen, Grok's or any of the other models.

sentences.zh: 我对此不确定。对我来说，这是同一类型的评论，例如评论指出，视觉模型的结构往往与视觉皮层的结构惊人地相似，即使没有专门尝试实现这一点，当然也是在我们对任何事物的理解范围内。因此，与其说是拟人化，不如说是对无意识仿生学的认识。虽然说实话，如果帖子中的任何图片看起来像我见过的任何儿童画，Grok 或任何其他模型，我会感到很遗憾。

blocks.html_en:
Both statements are true without contradiction.

A specific model develops as it passes through training.

AI labs change the architecture between models to allow them to surpass the previous models' best scores.

It's also going to keep being a hard sell to say "stop anthropomorphizing LLMs" when the models anthropomorphise themselves.

But besides that: Yeah, sure, they're not human, they're a cargo-cult mimicry of by and of minds that popped out of evolution doing gradient decent on intergenerational survivability. So what? Still interesting when the result of such cargo-culting incidentally echoes what our natural-selection-not-engineered moist electrochemistry happens to do.

sentences.en: Both statements are true without contradiction.A specific model develops as it passes through training.AI labs change the architecture between models to allow them to surpass the previous models' best scores.It's also going to keep being a hard sell to say "stop anthropomorphizing LLMs" when the models anthropomorphise themselves.But besides that: Yeah, sure, they're not human, they're a cargo-cult mimicry of by and of minds that popped out of evolution doing gradient decent on intergenerational survivability.
sentences.zh: 这两种说法都是正确的，没有矛盾。特定的模型在经过训练时会发展。人工智能实验室改变模型之间的架构，使它们超越以前模型的最佳分数。当模型将自己拟人化时，说“停止拟人化法学硕士”也将继续成为一种艰难的推销。但除此之外：是的，当然，他们不是人类，他们是一种对货物崇拜的模仿，是从进化中在代际上进行梯度体面的进化中突然出现的生存能力。

sentences.en: So what?
sentences.zh: 所以呢？

sentences.en: Still interesting when the result of such cargo-culting incidentally echoes what our natural-selection-not-engineered moist electrochemistry happens to do.
sentences.zh: 当这种货物崇拜的结果偶然呼应我们自然选择而不是工程设计的湿电化学恰好所做的事情时，仍然很有趣。

I do think they are going to stretch their lead in value if Anthropic doesn't wake up and stop YOLOing tokens. Kimi is an amazing achievement, but it has the same (or worse) kitchen sink approach as Fable.

At work, even if Fable is technically better I much prefer Sol because it is so much faster and concise.

blocks.html_en:
Grok 4.5 is...something else.

It performs much better than composer2.5 (while being as fast). It's not Opus, but I think it's not that far off. Definitely better than sonnet for what I've been doing.

On the other hand, I think they probably heavily adapted the training data so that it really is extremely focused on code. I just recently ran my personal "poetry benchmark" on it (where I give it ~850 poems I've written over my life and ask it to comment the corpus as a whole), and it's whack. It tries to write in portuguese (most of the poems are portuguese) and code-switches constantly and mixes up words to the point of making what it writes almost unreadable (e.g. it writes stuff like "You can't QoS that that look for beast poems", in portuguese, all messed up). The quality of the analysis is also quite bad (I'd say it's definitely behind Sonnet).

So I really think they either threw away data that wasn't tied to coding so that they could fine-tune it to that, or somehow they've got such an unbalanced dataset that coding ends up dominating either way. To me, its disastrous performance in this drawing "competition" fits this narrative.

blocks.html_en:
Some of my poetry has clear IT jargon, but it's a very small portion of it (<1%). Some of my teenage poetry revolved a lot around the idea of wanting to become science, knowledge, and machine, and be rid of feeling altogether (to become an idea that has no body, or to be come the mathematical equations that define the world), with some very amateur odes written glorifying science and machine (a clear pastiche of Álvaro de Campos with a modern twist). But, again, this is not the majority of the work, far from it.

For some reason, OpenAI models, Gemini (and apparently Grok too), love to latch onto this and obsess over this idea that it's "programming poetry" or "poetry for the IT crowd". Often OpenAI and Gemini try to write the "equations of my poetry" (granted, I do write about a cyclical relationship between thinking, feeling and writing a lot, and I do have ONE poem which ends with a Q.E.D.).

I'm giving this context to say that it is very bizarre. It's as if they latch onto it and act as if it's a core or highly distinguished part of the poetry, when it really isn't. Anthropic models, on the other hand, absolutely do not do this, and have never done it.

I really don't understand why this happens. Maybe it's because it has a lot of portuguese, I don't know. And even though the "QoS" is clearly the wrong token being generated, I have had situations where gemini spoke of some phase of my poetry as the "Q&A part" (really, no joke...)

In any case, it's why it's my personal benchmark after all :D

sentences.zh: OpenAI 和 Gemini 经常尝试写出“我的诗歌的方程式”（当然，我确实写了很多关于思考、感觉和写作之间的循环关系，而且我确实有一首以 Q.E.D. 结尾的诗）。我给出这个背景是为了说它非常奇怪。

sentences.en: Anthropic models, on the other hand, absolutely do not do this, and have never done it.I really don't understand why this happens.
sentences.zh: 另一方面，人择模型绝对不会这样做，也从来没有这样做过。我真的不明白为什么会发生这种情况。

sentences.en: Maybe it's because it has a lot of portuguese, I don't know.
sentences.zh: 也许是因为它有很多葡萄牙语，我不知道。

sentences.en: And even though the "QoS" is clearly the wrong token being generated, I have had situations where gemini spoke of some phase of my poetry as the "Q&A part" (really, no joke...)In any case, it's why it's my personal benchmark after all :D
sentences.zh: 即使“QoS”显然是生成的错误标记，我也遇到过双子座将我的诗歌的某些阶段称为“问答部分”的情况（真的，不是开玩笑......）无论如何，这就是为什么它毕竟是我的个人基准：D

blocks.html_en:
Yep, I did. It didn’t have any effect. Randomizing the poems has stopped having a significant effect in the last year or so on the analysis as a whole.

It still somewhat affects the way the AI looks at the poems, especially if it has to make lists of the “best”, but it is not a very pronounced effect nowadays. Maybe it gives some preference for earlier and latter poems but not a lot.

Back when I started doing it, these holes in the context were super obvious, just like you described. It would mixup entire periods and also hallucinate or forget about specific sections. That’s precisely why I started randomizing them as part of my experiments.

Nowadays the frontier models are much better and don’t really mix anything up.

blocks.html_en:
ESL?

Allow me to explain the theory behind the humor...

Grok's art had heavy use of what strongly resembles a permanent marker with lateral-ish scribbles similar to what one might see when someone is trying to mark something as discarded. Documents, especially those procured by the US government when responding to FOIA requests, contain many 'strike-through' style black marks resembling bold permanent-marker lines, which obfuscate the words they wish the reader to remain ignorant of. In many cases, this is the entire document, especially where unredacted words might otherwise serve in any way to clarify the nature of the FOIA inquiry, i.e. answer the question.

Grok's art, resembled to me, and others potentially appreciating the joke, redactions more than art. In some forms of humor, the presenter of the joke feigns obtuseness, or a mildly self-deprecating, stupid perspective. But the stupidity of the perspective actually reveals a relevant concept, or idea.

Often, such jokes can be deciphered using common sense, or various razors, including both occam and hanlon, the former self-suggesting that the joker is probably not so stupid as to think Grok was really redacting anything, and the latter self-suggesting that there was probably no malice involved.

sentences.en: ESL?Allow me to explain the theory behind the humor...Grok's art had heavy use of what strongly resembles a permanent marker with lateral-ish scribbles similar to what one might see when someone is trying to mark something as discarded.
sentences.zh: ESL？请允许我解释一下幽默背后的理论……Grok 的艺术大量使用了非常类似于永久性标记的东西，上面有横向的涂鸦，类似于当有人试图将某些东西标记为丢弃时人们可能会看到的东西。

sentences.en: Documents, especially those procured by the US government when responding to FOIA requests, contain many 'strike-through' style black marks resembling bold permanent-marker lines, which obfuscate the words they wish the reader to remain ignorant of.
sentences.zh: 文件，尤其是美国政府在回应《信息自由法》要求时获得的文件，包含许多类似粗体永久标记线的“删除线”式黑色标记，这些标记混淆了他们希望读者不知道的文字。

sentences.en: In many cases, this is the entire document, especially where unredacted words might otherwise serve in any way to clarify the nature of the FOIA inquiry, i.e. answer the question.Grok's art, resembled to me, and others potentially appreciating the joke, redactions more than art.
sentences.zh: 在许多情况下，这是整个文件，特别是在未编辑的文字可能以任何方式澄清 FOIA 调查的性质的情况下，即回答问题。Grok 的艺术，对我和其他可能欣赏这个笑话的人来说，编辑比艺术更重要。

sentences.en: In some forms of humor, the presenter of the joke feigns obtuseness, or a mildly self-deprecating, stupid perspective.
sentences.zh: 在某些形式的幽默中，笑话的讲述者会假装迟钝，或者表现出一种轻微的自嘲、愚蠢的观点。

sentences.en: But the stupidity of the perspective actually reveals a relevant concept, or idea.Often, such jokes can be deciphered using common sense, or various razors, including both occam and hanlon, the former self-suggesting that the joker is probably not so stupid as to think Grok was really redacting anything, and the latter self-suggesting that there was probably no malice involved.
sentences.zh: 但愚蠢的视角实际上揭示了一个相关的概念或想法。通常，这样的笑话可以用常识或各种剃刀来解读，包括奥卡姆和汉伦，前者自我暗示小丑可能不会愚蠢到认为格罗克真的在编辑任何东西，而后者自我暗示可能没有恶意。

blocks.html_en: Often, such jokes can be deciphered using common sense, or various razors, including both occam and hanlon, the former self-suggesting that the joker is probably not so stupid as to think Grok was really redacting anything, and the latter self-suggesting that there was probably no malice involved.

Opus doesn't have access to an image model, while ChatGPT does (it's not multi-modal, but it can prompt OpenAI's image model). So Opus by default is forced to write code to generate images, or generate SVG's as text. That's putting it in a worse situation than the linked article, where all the models were given rudimentary tools.

Give Opus access to an image model, and it will use that to create images just fine just like ChatGPT.

blocks.html_en:
The most interesting result for me is that they apparently prompted the models to optimize for SSIM, but many of the models trend worse over time. I suppose because viewing the canvas always comes after drawing, and they didn't give "revert to previous" capability as part of the toolkit.

Which in turn kinda jives with my experience of using these models for code: to some extent they only seem to have a concept of "forward", which invariably leads to "write more code to fix previous problems created", rather than taking a step back and removing broken things entirely.

sentences.en: The most interesting result for me is that they apparently prompted the models to optimize for SSIM, but many of the models trend worse over time.
sentences.zh: 对我来说最有趣的结果是，它们显然促使模型针对 SSIM 进行优化，但随着时间的推移，许多模型的趋势会变得更糟。

sentences.en: I suppose because viewing the canvas always comes after drawing, and they didn't give "revert to previous" capability as part of the toolkit.Which in turn kinda jives with my experience of using these models for code: to some extent they only seem to have a concept of "forward", which invariably leads to "write more code to fix previous problems created", rather than taking a step back and removing broken things entirely.
sentences.zh: 我想是因为查看画布总是在绘图之后进行，并且他们没有提供“恢复到以前的”功能作为工具包的一部分。这反过来又有点符合我使用这些模型编写代码的经验：在某种程度上，它们似乎只有“前进”的概念，这总是导致“编写更多代码来解决以前创建的问题”，而不是后退一步并完全删除损坏的东西。

Its rose is also insane. Not just the shading of the flower but its clever use of the smudge tool to soften the background and then soften the vase so it looked more like glass.

And I like how its starry night decided to accentuate the church/steeple in the village backdrop.

Pretty cool how it did these with a scrappy little tool palette. What a fun idea.

blocks.html_en: A year or so ago I was trying to get llms to make pixel art by asking them to create a 2d array of color codes. I'm no prompt guru but they all failed at anything beyond a basic shape. I tried at many resolutions from 16x16, 32x32, 64x64, and maybe a little larger but basically only then quit small ones were even recognizeable.

blocks.html_en: This is picking the wrong tool for the job twice over. Half of it is seeing if text-to-image mechanics exist in general purpose LLMs, the other half is seeing if they can reason their way about generating the 2d array. Imagine asking this of yourself and doing it through just a train of thought. Yes, I could probably draw a basic smiley face in the color of your choice in a 16x16 without antialiasing in 15 minutes.

sentences.en: This is picking the wrong tool for the job twice over.
sentences.zh: 这相当于两次为工作选择了错误的工具。

sentences.en: Half of it is seeing if text-to-image mechanics exist in general purpose LLMs, the other half is seeing if they can reason their way about generating the 2d array.
sentences.zh: 一半是看看通用法学硕士中是否存在文本到图像的机制，另一半是看看他们是否可以推理出生成二维数组的方法。

sentences.en: Imagine asking this of yourself and doing it through just a train of thought.
sentences.zh: 想象一下，你问自己这个问题，然后通过一系列的思考来做到这一点。

sentences.en: Yes, I could probably draw a basic smiley face in the color of your choice in a 16x16 without antialiasing in 15 minutes.
sentences.zh: 是的，我可能可以在 15 分钟内以您选择的颜色在 16x16 中绘制一个基本的笑脸，而无需抗锯齿。

blocks.html_en: Perhaps there was something in the prompt or settings preventing this, but I'm surprised (and slightly disappointed) that none of the models approached this the way I would: download an image of the Mona Lisa, run all of the drawing functions many times to construct a forward model of the drawing implements, and attempt to solve some kind of explicit inverse problem through either ML or a classical algorithm to minimize some difference metric. Were they just restricted from running code or are the models unindustrious without a very specific prompt?

sentences.en: Perhaps there was something in the prompt or settings preventing this, but I'm surprised (and slightly disappointed) that none of the models approached this the way I would: download an image of the Mona Lisa, run all of the drawing functions many times to construct a forward model of the drawing implements, and attempt to solve some kind of explicit inverse problem through either ML or a classical algorithm to minimize some difference metric.
sentences.zh: 也许提示或设置中存在某些内容阻止了这一点，但令我惊讶（并且有点失望）的是，没有一个模型按照我的方式实现这一点：下载蒙娜丽莎的图像，多次运行所有绘图函数以构建绘图工具的正向模型，并尝试通过 ML 或经典算法解决某种显式逆问题，以最小化某些差异度量。

blocks.html_en:
Weird choice of SSIM/RMSE. By feeding these back into the model, the agent is actively degrading its artistic output. This is only valid for the target reference I think?

Alternatively, I think much better results could be had by computing the cosine similarly using DINOv2 ONNX (@xenova/transformers).

My guess is that the results are greatly limited by relying on the current metrics.

Even better, attach toModelOutput directly to drawTool so it returns the rendered canvas image immediately upon drawing.

sentences.en: Weird choice of SSIM/RMSE.
sentences.zh: SSIM/RMSE 的奇怪选择。

sentences.en: By feeding these back into the model, the agent is actively degrading its artistic output.
sentences.zh: 通过将这些反馈到模型中，代理正在主动降低其艺术输出。

sentences.en: This is only valid for the target reference I think?Alternatively, I think much better results could be had by computing the cosine similarly using DINOv2 ONNX (@xenova/transformers).My guess is that the results are greatly limited by relying on the current metrics.Even better, attach toModelOutput directly to drawTool so it returns the rendered canvas image immediately upon drawing.
sentences.zh: 这仅对我认为的目标参考有效？或者，我认为通过使用 DINOv2 ONNX (@xenova/transformers) 类似地计算余弦可以获得更好的结果。我的猜测是，结果因依赖当前指标而受到很大限制。更好的是，将 toModelOutput 直接附加到 drawTool，以便它在绘制时立即返回渲染的画布图像。

blocks.html_en: I'm surprised that the harness wasn't better described here. Is the draw tool really just asking LLMs to provide a set of coordinates/parameters for generating shapes? Honestly, if so, the results are shockingly good. Think of how well a typical human artist would do with such a crude method of applying brushstrokes.

I think a lot better

blocks.html_en:
I'm missing some context here.

First of all, this is a marketing piece, made for engagement.

Second, in production, you would adjust your approach to the model's strengths and weaknesses. For example, if you found that a model does not respond to the tools you are offering, then you would find a way to make it more responsive. Also, we know that different models handle prompts and system prompts differently. So comparing several models from different providers against exactly the same prompt is quite a naive way of exploring the capabilities of those models or comparing them to each other. You may have just happened to “speak the language“ of one model better than that of another. Why would that be the model’s fault and not yours?

Third, none of these results mean anything if you do not run each process on each model several times to check for consistency. Your model might just have gotten lucky. This point is even more true when you're talking about a progressive task where one step builds on the previous one. The model might just have painted itself into a corner.

sentences.zh: 我在这里遗漏了一些背景信息。首先，这是一个营销作品，是为了参与而制作的。其次，在生产中，您将根据模型的优点和缺点调整方法。

sentences.en: For example, if you found that a model does not respond to the tools you are offering, then you would find a way to make it more responsive.
sentences.zh: 例如，如果您发现模型不响应您提供的工具，那么您会找到一种方法使其响应更快。

sentences.en: Also, we know that different models handle prompts and system prompts differently.
sentences.zh: 此外，我们知道不同的模型对提示和系统提示的处理方式不同。

sentences.en: So comparing several models from different providers against exactly the same prompt is quite a naive way of exploring the capabilities of those models or comparing them to each other.
sentences.zh: 因此，将来自不同提供商的多个模型与完全相同的提示进行比较是探索这些模型的功能或将它们相互比较的一种非常幼稚的方法。

sentences.en: You may have just happened to “speak the language“ of one model better than that of another.
sentences.zh: 您可能只是碰巧比另一种模型更好地“讲语言”。

sentences.en: Why would that be the model’s fault and not yours?Third, none of these results mean anything if you do not run each process on each model several times to check for consistency.
sentences.zh: 为什么这是模型的错，而不是你的错？第三，如果你不在每个模型上多次运行每个过程来检查一致性，那么这些结果都没有任何意义。

blocks.html_en:
I'm missing some context here.

First of all, this is a marketing piece, made for engagement.

Second, in production, you would adjust your approach to the model's strengths and weaknesses. For example, if you found that a model does not respond to the tools you are offering, then you would find a way to make it more responsive. Also, we know that different models handle prompts and system prompts differently. So comparing several models from different providers against exactly the same prompt is quite a naive way of exploring the capabilities of those models or comparing them to each other. You may have just happened to “speak the language“ of one model better than that of another. Why would that be the model’s fault and not yours?

Third, none of these results mean anything if you do not run each process on each model several times to check for consistency. Your model might just have gotten lucky. This point is even more true when you're talking about a progressive task where one step builds on the previous one. The model might just have painted itself into a corner.

content_html_zh: 当我浏览这些图像时，一开始我完全没有印象。 但是，然后我开始想，这些看起来有点...... 对我来说“幼稚”。幼稚如... 一个新的艺术家正在绘制一个概念而不是光/形式（这是艺术家通常会做的事情，因为他们越来越了解绘画）。特别是花瓶中的玫瑰 - 一些模型理解应该有阴影，反射，折射的概念 - 其他人只是画“蓝色 = 玻璃”和“绿色 = 茎”和“红色 = 玫瑰”。看起来真的很奇怪，考虑到如果我看到任何来自人类孩子的这些图画，我会说“干得好，伙计”并将其放在冰箱。 我预计随着模型的改进，这些会变得更好，也许艺术进步也会随之而来......我称之为“符号绘制”。 初学者艺术家都会这样做。 他们认为“这是一个头，头是圆的。 这就是眼睛所去的地方，眼睛的形状是这样的”，整个事情最终成为符号的拼贴画，而不是空间的表示和观看脸部的体验。 以前我画得还可以，那是因为我强迫自己用触觉而不是视觉来构图。 解释起来很奇怪，但我觉得 3D 可以得到照明等更好的东西。比我聪明的人告诉我欣赏的很多艺术似乎都遵循击中空间和/或体验的模式，同时最大限度地减少符号的使用。 印象派绘画尤其避免使用符号，恕我直言，而毕加索则完全滥用符号，但仍然达到了他们想要的体验。看起来真的很奇怪，考虑到如果我看到人类孩子的这些图画，我会说“干得好，伙计”，然后把它放在冰箱上。Grok 的那些画尤其给了我这样的想法。 其中大多数看起来确实像孩子在使用相同工具时会做的事情，而其他模型的输出对它们来说明显更具“人工智能”（因为缺乏更好的术语。）我对 Gemini 采取了同样的提示，并对结果感到震惊。 它们与文章中显示的图像（以及生成的真正好的艺术作品）完全不同。您使用的是 Gemini 图像生成器吗？ 这篇文章是关于使用 LLM 通过工具调用进行绘图的。有趣的是，他们的孩子气实际上是非常人性化的。 事实上，人们经常教你如何更真实地绘画的方法之一就是停止将概念视为你正在绘制轮廓的图标，而是模糊你的眼睛并看到事物的本来面目：色调和明度。 换句话说，成为一台相机或打印机，除了值网格之外，不知道它正在捕获或打印什么。 这就是实现现实主义的方法。事实上，它已经在头脑中清楚地形象化了这些概念，并正在追踪它认为/期望去往的事物的轮廓，这是非常人性化的。是的——这正是我幼稚的评论所要表达的意思。看到机器演绎的东西与年轻人探索和了解更多世界时非常接近的东西，感觉有点超现实。 甚至（对我来说）也很可怕。 这是我第一次看到法学硕士的输出，并想到“哇，也许这是在学习。”鹦鹉的声音也可以非常人性化，但这只是模仿。我们如何知道其中的区别？是的，但是鹦鹉不会像孩子那样模仿学习语言发展的阶段。 他们只是记住一个短语。这些 SOTA 法学硕士并没有试图模仿现有的儿童绘画，但有趣的是，他们在成长过程中遵循了与人类儿童相似的进展。他们不发展。 我们应该停止将法学硕士拟人化。人工智能实验室正在改进用于构建更好模型的机器学习技术；这是一个很大的区别。这也是他们所指的非常明确的，或者至少我肯定怀疑他们或这里的大多数人在这一点上是否会迷失这一点。我想现在在座的每个人都应该清楚这一点，直到我读到这样的帖子，将儿童发展与法学硕士建设的进步进行比较。这就像说新的 M-Apple 芯片像孩子一样开发。 法学硕士有一种明显的倾向，即赋予其类似人类的属性，这可能是由于机器学习中使用的术语造成的：训练、学习等。我对此不确定。对我来说，这是同一类型的评论，例如评论指出，视觉模型的结构往往与视觉皮层的结构惊人地相似，即使没有专门尝试实现这一点，当然也是在我们对任何事物的理解范围内。因此，与其说是拟人化，不如说是对无意识仿生学的认识。虽然说实话，如果帖子中的任何图片看起来像我见过的任何儿童画，Grok 或任何其他模型，我会感到很遗憾。法学硕士完成后，他们不会根据与世界的互动进行即时培训。 如果法学硕士不再允许编辑，并且之前的所有编辑都是训练，那么他们就是维基百科。这两种说法都是正确的，没有矛盾。特定的模型在经过训练时会发展。人工智能实验室改变模型之间的架构，使它们超越以前模型的最佳分数。当模型将自己拟人化时，说“停止拟人化法学硕士”也将继续成为一种艰难的推销。但除此之外：是的，当然，他们不是人类，他们是一种对货物崇拜的模仿，是从进化中在代际上进行梯度体面的进化中突然出现的生存能力。 所以呢？ 当这种货物崇拜的结果偶然呼应我们自然选择而不是工程设计的湿电化学恰好所做的事情时，仍然很有趣。如果你想知道某个东西是人还是鹦鹉，你可以给它一把画笔，让它画《蒙娜丽莎》。你也可以用你的眼睛、手或其他感官:')。让我想起那些笑话？鹦鹉和人类有什么区别？ 沉默rly？ 你分不清鹦鹉和人类之间的区别吗？ :')我的鹦鹉没有台词，但它肯定可以重复在 Reddit 上听到的内容。那么，你是说是 Reddit 上的某人反驳了这个猜想，而不是“随机鹦鹉”？你问的是我的鹦鹉的看法，而不是如何反驳一个猜想。编辑：考虑到人类没有反驳这个猜想，这不是一个很好的例子来证明人性吗？GPT 5.6 Sol 拥有最好的两张图（玫瑰和星夜），但更令人印象深刻的是它与《神鬼寓言》相比 RE 成本/时间/代币的效率（3.4M vs 14.6M / $7.74 vs $161！）。 OpenAI 围绕推理悄然进行创新——即使与开放模型相比，这也将成为一个日益增长的差异化因素。我确实认为，如果 Anthropic 不醒来并停止 YOLO 代币，他们将扩大其价值领先地位。 Kimi 是一项了不起的成就，但它与《Fable》有相同（或更差）的厨房水槽方法。 在工作中，即使《Fable》在技术上更好，我也更喜欢 Sol，因为它更快、更简洁。就我个人而言，使用比中级或有时高级更多的推理是没有意义的。 它变得如此缓慢和昂贵，这意味着我必须等待更长的时间来评估结果并提供反馈或继续下一个任务。天哪！ 哈哈！说真的，那里发生了什么事？ 为何与其他人如此不同？ 它只是在技术/培训方面落后，还是使用了根本不同的东西？Grok 4.5 是...别的东西。它的性能比composer2.5 好得多（同时速度也一样快）。 这不是 Opus，但我认为也不是那么遥远。 就我一直在做的事情而言，绝对比十四行诗更好。另一方面，我认为他们可能大量调整了训练数据，因此它确实非常专注于代码。 我最近刚刚在它上面运行了我个人的“诗歌基准”（我给了它我一生写的大约 850 首诗，并要求它对整个语料库进行评论），结果很糟糕。 它尝试用葡萄牙语写作（大多数诗歌都是葡萄牙语），不断地进行语码转换，混淆单词，以致于它所写的内容几乎无法阅读（例如，它用葡萄牙语写了“你不能保证寻找野兽诗的服务质量”之类的东西，全都搞砸了）。 分析的质量也很糟糕（我想说它肯定落后于十四行诗）。所以我真的认为他们要么扔掉与编码无关的数据，以便他们可以对其进行微调，要么不知何故他们得到了如此不平衡的数据集，以至于编码最终在任何一种情况下都占主导地位。 对我来说，它在这次绘画“比赛”中的灾难性表现符合这个叙述。我的一些诗歌有清晰的 IT 术语，但这只是其中的一小部分（<1%）。 我青少年时期的一些诗歌主要围绕着这样的想法：想要成为科学、知识和机器，并完全摆脱感觉（成为一个没有实体的想法，或者成为定义世界的数学方程），其中有一些非常业余的颂歌，颂扬科学和机器（显然是阿尔瓦罗·德·坎波斯的现代风格的模仿）。 但是，再说一次，这并不是大部分工作，远非如此。出于某种原因，OpenAI 模型、Gemini（显然还有 Grok）喜欢抓住这一点，并痴迷于“编程诗歌”或“IT 人群的诗歌”这一想法。 OpenAI 和 Gemini 经常尝试写出“我的诗歌的方程式”（当然，我确实写了很多关于思考、感觉和写作之间的循环关系，而且我确实有一首以 Q.E.D. 结尾的诗）。我给出这个背景是为了说它非常奇怪。 就好像他们紧紧抓住它，表现得好像它是诗歌的核心或高度杰出的部分，但实际上并非如此。 另一方面，人择模型绝对不会这样做，也从来没有这样做过。我真的不明白为什么会发生这种情况。 也许是因为它有很多葡萄牙语，我不知道。 即使“QoS”显然是生成的错误标记，我也遇到过双子座将我的诗歌的某些阶段称为“问答部分”的情况（真的，不是开玩笑......）无论如何，这就是为什么它毕竟是我的个人基准：D也可能是U形记忆问题，你是否尝试改变你的诗的顺序，看看它是否集中在不同的诗上？ 也许它只是碰巧在记忆集中的地方有那些旧的。 （ofc 不好，但可以作为它的替代想法，专注于 IT 事物/代码来产生这样的结果）是的，我做到了。 它没有任何效果。 在过去一年左右的时间里，对诗歌进行随机化已经不再对整个分析产生显着影响。它仍然在一定程度上影响人工智能看待诗歌的方式，特别是如果它必须列出“最好”的列表，但现在这并不是一个非常明显的影响。 也许它对早期和晚期的诗歌给予了一些偏好，但不是很多。当我开始这样做时，上下文中的这些漏洞非常明显，就像你所描述的那样。 它会混淆整个时期，也会产生幻觉或忘记特定的部分。 这正是我开始将它们随机化作为实验的一部分的原因。现在，前沿模型要好得多，并且不会真正混淆任何东西。Elon 有没有告诉某个可怜的工程师给 grok 一个快速的绘图注入，“让它看起来像我童年的画作之一！” 就像特斯拉卡车一样？《星夜》底部的铁丝网很巧妙，而且非常格洛克。 真正展现了它的军事精神。编辑：我只是不明白编辑蒙娜丽莎的意义这不是一个近乎无限迟钝的人吗？HN，世界上唯一一个人们可以科学地伪造笑话的环境。ESL？请允许我解释一下幽默背后的理论……Grok 的艺术大量使用了非常类似于永久性标记的东西，上面有横向的涂鸦，类似于当有人试图将某些东西标记为丢弃时人们可能会看到的东西。 文件，尤其是美国政府在回应《信息自由法》要求时获得的文件，包含许多类似粗体永久标记线的“删除线”式黑色标记，这些标记混淆了他们希望读者不知道的文字。 在许多情况下，这是整个文件，特别是在未编辑的文字可能以任何方式澄清 FOIA 调查的性质的情况下，即回答问题。Grok 的艺术，对我和其他可能欣赏这个笑话的人来说，编辑比艺术更重要。 在某些形式的幽默中，笑话的讲述者会假装迟钝，或者表现出一种轻微的自嘲、愚蠢的观点。 但愚蠢的视角实际上揭示了一个相关的概念或想法。通常，这样的笑话可以用常识或各种剃刀来解读，包括奥卡姆和汉伦，前者自我暗示小丑可能不会愚蠢到认为格罗克真的在编辑任何东西，而后者自我暗示可能没有恶意。我把这些球理解为“房子”，尽管从它们的正中心出现的阳具尖顶也非常品牌化我必须承认，我有一种自然的倾向，会忽视 C&B，但确实存在——它就在那里。 也许这可以解释《蒙娜丽莎》的修订； Grok 可能在这方面投入了更多的精力。Grok 的那些很有趣，几乎是可笑的糟糕。 然而，每当我尝试将图像创建请求传递给任何 Opus 模型时，情况都会更糟，就像使用 Microsoft Paint 的第一周很糟糕（而 ChatGPT 会使用相同的提示创建社交媒体质量的图像）Opus 无法访问图像模型，而 ChatGPT
